full_response:
Below we work in units G=c=1 and follow the usual Kruskal‐Szekeres construction.  We show that the hypersurface r=0 in (U,V)–space is given by UV=1, and that this hyperbola splits into two disconnected branches, one lying in region II (the black‐hole future singularity) and one in region IV (the white‐hole past singularity).

1.  Schwarzschild “tortoise” coordinate and null coordinates  
   Define  
     r*  :=  r + 2M ln|r/2M − 1|,  
     u := t − r*,    v := t + r*.  
   In region I (r>2M) one sets  
     U := − e^(−u/4M),    V :=   e^(+v/4M),  
   so that U<0, V>0 there.  By analytic continuation one uses the same definitions in all four regions, yielding the causal diamond with   
     Region I:  U<0, V>0  
     Region II: U>0, V>0  
     Region III: U>0, V<0  
     Region IV: U<0, V<0.

2.  Relation UV↔r  
   Since v−u = 2r*,  
     UV = (−e^(−u/4M)) (e^(v/4M))  
        = − e^((v−u)/4M)  
        = − e^(r*/2M)  
        = (1 − r/2M) e^(r/2M)    [because e^(r*/2M) = e^(r/2M) |r/2M−1| ].  
   One checks that in region II (r<2M) the quantity (1−r/2M) is positive, so the formula can be written uniformly as  
     UV = (1 − r/(2M)) e^(r/(2M)).  

3.  The singularity r=0  
   Set r=0 in the above relation to get  
     UV  =  (1 − 0) e^0  =  1.  
   Hence the locus r=0 in the Kruskal plane is the hyperbola  
     UV = 1.  

4.  Two disconnected branches  
   UV=1 implies U and V have the same sign.  Thus there are two branches:  
     (i)  U>0, V>0  ⇒  region II.  
     (ii) U<0, V<0  ⇒  region IV.  

5.  Physical interpretation  
   •  In region II (U>0,V>0) an infalling observer reaches r=0 in *finite* proper time to his future ⇒ this is the black‐hole (future) singularity.  
   •  In region IV (U<0,V<0) a timelike curve can be traced back from region IV into r=0 in its *past* ⇒ this is the white‐hole (past) singularity.  

∴  r(U,V)=0 ⇔ UV=1 splits into two disconnected singularities, one lying in the future of region II (black hole) and one in the past of region IV (white hole).

\boxed{%
\text{In Kruskal coordinates }UV=(1-\tfrac r{2M})e^{r/(2M)},\;
r=0\iff UV=1,
\text{ giving two branches }
(U>0,V>0)\text{ (future singularity in II) and }
(U<0,V<0)\text{ (past singularity in IV).}
}
  
Confidence: 95%
